text: The surgeon encounters plastered small bowel adhesions in the pelvis, obscuring the view of the pelvic region. The adhesions involve a loop of small bowel along the right iliac vessels, demonstrating the challenges of minimally invasive surgery in this case.
The surgeon uses robotic technology to lysive adhesions, with a loop of small bowel plastered to the bladder on the right side, visible above the surgeon's left hand.

The surgeon navigates complex lysive adhesions in the pelvic region using robotic technology. A loop of small bowel is adhered to the right dome of the bladder, with another loop positioned directly behind it. The left dome of the bladder and the colon are also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the small bowel to expose the pelvis, revealing the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon and additional loops of small bowel that require freeing. A cavity is visible, with pus indicating interloop abscesses not drained by the interventional radiology drain.

The surgeon uses cold scissors and blunt pushing to carefully dissect and separate a loop of small bowel from the surrounding rind. The pelvis remains obscured by adhesions, complicating the visualization and resection of the colon.

The surgeon separates a loop of small bowel from the rectosigmoid mesentery at the pelvic brim. The uterus is visible below, with the small bowel loop previously adhered beneath it. Residual pus is observed from the abscess cavity. The deeper pelvis is exposed, revealing the right ureter as the colon is lifted. The surgeon continues to free the remaining small bowel.

The surgeon identifies the cecum, appendix, and terminal ileum, mobilizing the terminal ileum mesentery. The small bowel is now fully mobilized, allowing a view into the pelvis where a loop of sigmoid colon is observed flopped into the deep pelvis. The colon is visibly adhered to the left dome of the bladder.

The surgeon uses the robotic arm to separate the colon from the uterus, revealing the anterior pelvis. The left tube and ovary are preserved during the procedure. The IR drain is visible in the posterior left pelvis, showing a cleaned-up abscess area. The uterus is suspended to improve access and visualization of the pelvic region.

The surgeon navigates into the deep anterior pelvis, where a loop of sigmoid colon is flopped over, aiming to identify the true rectum. This exploration is crucial for deciding whether to perform an anastomosis or divert it.

The surgeon manipulates the right lateral mesorectum, which is adherent to the anterior portion of the colon. Suction is used to clear blood from the surgical field. The procedure is performed using four ports, with the camera positioned in arm three, allowing the surgeon to work with two left hands.

The surgeon navigates the deeper pelvis using robotic arm number four, with scissors occasionally contacting the iliac vessels. The camera is port-hopped to optimize the view and avoid obstruction. The surgeon encounters difficulty with a portion of tissue that is stuck, requiring careful manipulation to achieve a better angle. A scope is used to check for the anterior reflection, but a loop of colon remains flopped over the cul-de-sac, which has yet to be identified.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection in the pelvis, identifying the left ureter as a landmark. A small hole is visible in the mesentery as the surgeon follows the ureter down into the pelvis, moving the rectosigmoid off the sidewall to access an unviolated plane.

The surgeon joins the dissection medially and lifts the mesorectum posteriorly to access the deep pelvis. This maneuver facilitates better manipulation of the bowel, which is adhered in the pelvic region, allowing for improved visualization and access.

The surgeon identifies the colon adhered to the right lateral mesorectum and begins cutting through the tissue with precision, ensuring not to injure the anterior wall of the rectum. The dissection reveals a clear plane, allowing the surgeon to elevate the rectum further out of the pelvis for better access.

The surgeon separates the anterior portion of the colon from the cul-de-sac, revealing the anterior rectum that has been exposed to an abscess cavity. The tissue appears inflamed, influencing the decision to avoid an anastomosis.

The surgeon works to mobilize the remaining colon to facilitate division of the rectum. The anterior wall of the rectum appears damaged. A scope is used to confirm the absence of colon flopped over the cul-de-sac, revealing a clear view. The surgeon marks the site for colostomy creation and performs lateral mobilization to ensure adequate reach. The pelvic drain is removed.

The surgeon removes the pelvic drain, addressing minor bleeding with a sponge. The mesentery is dissected, joining two spots near the bowel wall above the superior hemorrhoidal artery. The specimen is extracted through the marked colostomy site, avoiding a new incision, with plans to close the site during Hartmann reversal.

The surgeon cleans off the rectal mesentery, ensuring the dissection is below the loop of tissue previously flopped over the top. Due to the diseased and adherent sigmoid colon, the surgeon resects just beneath the recto-sigmoid junction, performs a perfusion assessment, and divides the upper rectum.

The surgeon creates a left upper quadrant colostomy, using a stapler introduced through arm three, with the camera positioned in arm two for a different view. The rectal staple line is marked with a proline suture, and a leak test is performed on the rectal stump following anterior dissection near the cul-de-sac.
